entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Now it works.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Okay, perfect. Sounds good.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Okay. Wow.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah. So these are aubergines, and then next door we have a lot of greens. So, yeah, Louv, I can start over if you like. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So Louf was founded in 2009 by four co founders. One of them is still our founder and CEO, and he's called Mo. He's originally from Lebanon, and when he was in Lebanon, he found that there was a complete transparency on the food chain. He knew exactly where his food was coming from, who was producing it, how it was produced. And then when he came to Montreal, he found it very difficult to find that transparency, find the information, understand how the food system functions, where the food that we eat comes from. And so he had kind of this crazy idea of growing food where people live. And that's where the idea of rooftop farming came from initially. So in 2010, 2011, this greenhouse was built. And it was a bit of a difficult project to start at the beginning because it had never really been done before on such a big scale, and not everyone believed in it. But once we built this greenhouse and proved that this model is very viable, we had a lot more support for building all of the other greenhouses. So now we have five greenhouses, including another one, Marche Centrale, across the way over there. This is the last one we've built, and it's very advanced in terms of technology. This is our first one. It still produces an impressive amount of food, but the technology used here isn't as maybe impressive as the one over there, but it still functions super well. So here at the moment, we have aubergines, and then here we have horizontal farming, so it's more herbs and greens. We built this greenhouse with two temperature zones and two different types of cultures growing methods, because we wanted to be able to give as much variety to our clients in the beginning as we possibly could. But it's a bit of a headache because two temperature zones means one hydroponic system, one climate control system, and two different, very different types of growing. So we have to kind of balance that. But it allowed us in the beginning to offer tomatoes as well as lettuce and fresh herbs. So that's how we started in 2013, we built a greenhouse at the same time as we built, or as the developer built, the building below. So we partnered with a real estate developer who built a building in Laval, and we were put in touch, and they were interested in having a greenhouse on top of it. And it's a great model for us because it means we don't have to retrofit a building. We can have the greenhouse and everything that we need in the plans. Initially, something really simple that people don't think about is having an elevator shaft that goes up to the roof.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: This building was particular because there was supposed to be a third floor that was never built. And so the elevator does come up to the roof. Exactly. But yeah, the owner of this building wasn't convinced at the beginning. It's one of my favorite anecdotes. But he said no to us when we asked if we could rent the roof to build a greenhouse. And that evening he talked to his wife and his daughter about the project, and they both said that they thought it was a really cool project. And. And so he called us back to tell us that he was interested in it.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Love some good women.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: We love some good women with a vision. Exactly.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Imagination.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Absolutely.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I imagine this is difficult to get people on board with, especially in the beginning, because it's so different.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: You know, it's a hard sell. I think it was a hard sell. Now it's become kind of taken for granted that this is possible. Possible and doable and viable. And, you know, you can have a for profit company as well as be super conscious of the environment and food chains and transparency and all of that. So we Loofah is kind of proving that you can have both. Because we are a for profit company. Yeah. But, yeah, I think in the beginning people thought it was a kind of crazy idea.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. And how does it work with all the supplies you guys need? It must be easier almost to get it in the city.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It depends on the suppliers. We have one distribution center. That's where all the food comes and then is packed into reusable baskets, and then it goes out that same day that it's packed. And for most of our products, or maybe not most, but a lot of our products, we sell everything before it's harvested or produced. So the way that our online marketplace works, we don't have any physical retail space. It's all online. You create an account, decide on a delivery day, and you have until midnight the night before your delivery day to make your basket. So three days before your delivery day, your marketplace opens, and you have until midnight to customize your basket or cancel it. And at midnight, all of the orders are automatically sent out to our greenhouses and to our partners. So bakers, butchers, people who prepare fresh salads, all sent during the night to them. The products are prepared during the night. So the bread is baked, then it's sent around 5, 6am to a distribution centre, the baskets start being packed and that same day the baskets are sent out to. So it reduces food waste because we're not over producing. We're only producing what's already been sold and we're harvesting what's already been sold. And it means that we can offer a level of freshness that you really don't get anywhere else.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay, so you also work with a lot of other producers essentially. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: The idea is to make it really easy for people to support local producers, have the freshest food around, because it's tough sometimes to want to make the good choices, but you don't have the time, you don't have the mental headspace. If you're a mum and you have three little children and you have a job and yet you have a partner maybe, but you're thinking about so many things and you're juggling so many things. You don't have time to run around town to your local butchers, to your local cheesemaker, to your local bakery, even if you want to support those people because they're part of your community. So what we've done is found a way to make that really easy for people to support their local community, to make an impact all round and make, you know, good choices, all while not having the headache of having to like, do the logistics of it all.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Okay, and how did that those partnerships start? How did you.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So in the beginning we only had this greenhouse and the Laval greenhouse, and we had a customer service team, I think one or two people that kept getting calls from clients saying, hey, we'd really like, like potatoes in our basket. Can you guys get potatoes? Oh, we really love carrots. Can you guys grow carrots? Like, well, no, we can't grow carrots, but maybe someone else produces carrots and we share values. And so it kind of got built bit by bit through client demand. And then we develop all of our IT stuff internally. And so we developed the marketplace with maybe 50 products at the beginning. And then it just got more and more and more and more populated. And in the end it ended up being, you know, a whole grocery store online and it supports local farmers. I was talking to, by a complete happenstance, but a young woman who is studying agriculture at McGill University. And she was talking to farmers and she was being told by the farmers how easy it is to work with loofah because they can just focus on farming. They don't have to focus on the logistics, the admin, the selling, they just sell to loofah. We Sell to clients, and it's super easy for them. And they don't have to necessarily have to make the choice between selling themselves, selling to a really big distributor. That's going to be quite difficult with negotiations and everything. So, yeah, that was lovely feedback to hear from the farmers and to students, which was nice. So we're very aware that we often say that it has to be win, win, win. It can't just be win, win as we win for our clients, win win for us, but also win for the producers that we work with. We wouldn't have the marketplace without them, and it has to be a collaborative effort.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay, that's very cool. So it's both almost a hub for local producers that share the same values, but also a place for you guys to sell your own produce.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Our own produce. And we only sell B2C. We don't sell any B2B. Okay. Yeah. So it's direct to consumer. We're trying to shorten the supply chain as much as possible.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. And what are you thinking around this? I can probably guess, but I would love to hear.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: What do you mean?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So why do you want to shorten this supply chain?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Oh, we want to shorten the supply chain because it makes sense in terms of people's nutrition, in terms of the environment. It's the logical step. But we have the capacity to produce a lot of food where people live, no matter where they live, more or less in the world. With the technology that we have in our world today and people's creativity and their determination and adaptability, there is very little reason to have a food system where you're eating food that isn't fresh, has been produced in a way that isn't ethical and terms of a human side of things, but also an environmental side of things half a world away. And by the. It's picked unripe. It's not at peak nutritious value, doesn't taste like anything. It spends days, sometimes weeks on transport, you know, on trucks, on planes, on boats even sometimes. And it gets to a grocery store and it's barely good anymore. But somehow that's the cheapest option on the market. Because in different countries, far, far away, where we don't think about it, labor laws are maybe a lot more lax and they don't protect people, and there's abuse and it's cheaper. But there's no reason in a very, very foreseeable future that food that's produced here rivals that cost as well, and that it's better. I think the struggle that people have at the moment is they have a budget and they are put in a position where they have to make a choice between buying something that's locally produced, organic, synthetic, pesticide free, but maybe slightly more expensive, and produce that's extremely cheap but not necessarily as good quality because they're on that budget. We're trying to change that. With every greenhouse that we build, we become more efficient, our costs come down, we benefit from economies of scale. And the dream is to get to a point where a family doesn't have to choose between local cucumbers and cucumbers from Mexico because of something as kind of complex as price. We want to be able to rival that so that they're not in that position in the first place. Because I think when people are given the opportunity to make better choices for their own families, but also for the environment, they do it.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, when they can.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: When they can. Exactly. So we want to. It's a system that's broken. It's not people's choices.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. I saw you studied psychology as well.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah. Oh, I loved it. It was such an incredible experience. And it's how also that kind of idea that it's not people that are broken in systems comes into a lot of our logistics as well. At Loofah, we firmly believe that if you have a good system, you minimize human error and things become slightly more, you know, you kind of go on autopilot so you make less errors. And we've seen that in our distribution center a couple of years ago, we moved, and the space is a lot bigger, It's a lot more organized, and our error rates are. Are extremely low because the system works well. And then people just have to do the motions within the system. And it means that the error rates are lower, our clients are more satisfied, our team members get better bonuses. So it just works for everyone. So we're very big on systems at Loofah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Oh, you would love the master that I've just taken. It's called Psychology of Economic Life, and it's all about, well, analyzing and creating systems that facilitate the behavior we need. So sustainable behavior in kind of the broadest sense of the word.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: I would love that. That sounds incredible.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: The professor who runs this study is the professor from London who's just written a book about systems. Like you just talked about it. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Amazing.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: This is also what we're seeing.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Where was it in London?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: London School of Economics. Okay, nice.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Wow. Lsu.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Very cool. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Oh, I love that. There are a few, like, corporate psychology masters I looked at in London. I Was on a goldsmiths as well. And then I started at Loofah and I've never left. One day it'll happen. One day.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Well, you can also do a lot of self study.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Oh, I'd love that. Spread the word. Yeah. But yeah, I think it might be a little naive, but I do think people, you know, intrinsically want to make good choices.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Well, I agree, I personally, it's naive.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: But I also don't want to not believe it.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes. So I kind of don't care.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, exactly.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: But I also think it's true.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: I think it's true and I feel like my life has. And the things I've witnessed have proven that it is true. Until something is proven otherwise, then I'll keep believing it. It's based on empirical data, completely scientific, not stubborn at all. I wish I could show you the more technologically advanced one as well, but they're in a massive plant pool. They're rotating cult. So it's empty. Yeah. You'll have to come back to Montreal.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes. I. I love it though.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Have you. Is it your first time?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: It is my first time.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Oh, wonderful.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes. I'm seeing this concert, you know.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yes. Are you here on your own?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: No, I am. I'm here to visit.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Ah, love. Ah, perfect.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: There's Les Francourts at the moment with a lot of free concerts and I recommend you go to the concert on Thursday night at 9pm we're going on a van. Oh no. Where are you going?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: We're going up coast.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: To Quebec City.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Lovely.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: And then you're going to see some whales in Kaiser.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, exactly.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Oh, wonderful.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Have you always lived here?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: No, no, my mom's British.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah. I was born in London.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Ah, you were? Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: And then when I was six, we moved to France and then I lived in St. Petersburg for a couple of years and then Montreal for university. And during firstly the pandemic happened, I lost my job as a waitress and started working at Loofah. And then. Yeah, never left. Well, it's been five years.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Good run with the company as well.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, absolutely.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So how does it work in the company? Because you must have been there since the beginning, huh?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: No. So Loofah started 15 years ago. It's our 15th anniversary this year. Yeah. So it's very exciting. But I started, yeah. Five years ago when we hit, just built the biggest greenhouse that we have, which is also the world's biggest rooftop greenhouse. It's 163,000 square feet.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Wow.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So it's about three football fields.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: That's really big.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah. This one is 30, some 36, 38,000 square feet. So it's like four times this. And it's in Bunt Laurent on top of the building where we have our distribution center. We have an indoor farm as well. So completely climate controlled environment. And then our offices, main office.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay. Yeah, but it's quite a small team. Do you like know each other and like run away?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: The corporate team is quite, is quite small. Everyone knows each other. We are in the office relatively often. More so nowadays people are going in a bit more. We have corporate events. We had a breakfast yesterday. I couldn't go. But I organize. I manage a lot of the internal events as well. So we try and do a put block or go bowling or have yeah, some cohesion within the team. But we're a little siloed when it comes to like greenhouse distribution center. Corporate because the greenhouses are all over the city so we don't necessarily see the people that are working in them. And then the distribution center, there's a lot of people part time. I'm very close for example with the management team because we work on a lot of projects together. But the actual people who pack the baskets and stuff, a lot of students, a lot of part time and so they're just in and out to do their shifts. But we do try and have an event like there's a Christmas party every year where everyone's invited and that's really fun. And then in the summer we try and do some like food truck events and that kind of stuff to get everyone together.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Nice.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, so it is good. Could be a bit more work to be done there. But people are also busy. They don't necessarily want to stay after work, which is completely understandable. So we try and do the events during the day.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: How does it work with decisions? Who makes the decision?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It's depending on the department but it's fairly collaborative. But then top down. So Mo, who's our founder and CEO, is very, very involved in the day to day, whether it be logistics or new projects. His excitement for new projects is really contagious, which is really lovely. So he's often in the office working with different teams. He's also very interested and very involved obviously with the greenhouses. So he kind of, I think since the beginning has very much guided the vision process and the like the journey of the company and then gone to find the expertise, gone to find the people to support that vision, gone to like build this team that's very solid and super like knowledgeable about the different areas of the company because you know, it's not a company that just does engineering. It's a company where we have engineering, we have marketing, we have corporate psychology with hr, we have entire IT department because we build everything in their house. So it's almost like being a CEO of loads of little different companies and loads of different things. And I think he is very active in all of that decision making process, which is nice as well. So you don't necessarily feel too much distance, it's not too hierarchical, which is good.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So he's also a bit of a network here.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, absolutely.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Cool. Yeah. Because we're super fascinated by, you know, like systemic transition and kind of the widest scale and then how relationships and non monetary transactions really push systems forward.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So you're collaborating partners and like how these relationship functions. I'd be very interested to hear a bit more about because from what you're saying now it sounds like. What was his name again?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: The CEO. Yeah, Mohammed.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Mohammed. Okay. It sounds like he's very good at getting a relationship to work well.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: He's very personable. He is also. He very much is the kind of person and I think it's quite a rare quality that very much has a vision.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: And can see the end goal very clearly and knows where he wants to go, knows where he wants to be and is like very secure in that and then is like very good at going to find the people that can help him get to that point. And I think that's what happened with the first greenhouse and then has continued to happen with Loofah in general. It's like, okay, we're here, we want to be here. How do we get that? And so we have a very big purchasing department compared to other food companies. Like there's a company over there, for example, they have smaller purchasing departments but everything is automated. Everything. Like they all have the same quantities of products that are bought every week. Whereas we work with small, smaller producers and we work with smaller farmers, for example, that might, you know, if they have a crop failure then we have to find someone else. And so but while keeping that relationship for the year after or for if they have strawberries and the strawberries are doing really well, then we can sell the strawberries. And it's kind of that interpersonal relationship, I think, with our partners that make it work as well. Because our vision is very much of a partnership. We can't afford to be in a situation where, you know, we only use those partners when we need them and then risk not having them the year after. We have to maintain these partnerships.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: How do you do that?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: We do it through very much. Through the purchasing team. A lot of negotiation, a lot of human contact and phone calls and conversations and then having people on hand to be able to take those calls and answer the emails and find ways to. To work. Yeah. Collaboratively. Instead of it being, we need this. Can you provide it? No. Fine. We'll go on to someone else.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It's like, okay, we need this. Can you provide it? No, but I have. I don't have sweet potatoes right now. I have courgettes. Great. We'll take them kind of like. Yeah, exactly. And for us to show an adaptability as well. Our greenhouses at the moment are for. Tomatoes are in rotation. So we don't currently have as many tomatoes on the marketplace as we'd want. But it's okay because. Because it's the start of Quebec field season, so the fields have tomatoes. And so we try and stack our crop rotations when the summer comes so that we can sell products that are from the fields, from our partner farms. Instead of being in competition. Not in competition, but for clients to make the choice between loofah tomatoes or someone else's tomatoes. There just aren't loofah tomatoes on the marketplace at the moment because as a field season, to be supported and encouraged and had as well. So it's that kind of collaborative side of things.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, that makes a lot of sense. I love that. Just make sure. And this is this greenhouse, right? We've kind of seen it. No.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So this. Yeah, this is it for. For the greenhouse in terms of. I mean, there's the growing racks and there's.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Can we have a look at.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, of course. Go for it like that.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And you have light. Yes.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So these are the germination tables. It's where we seed. So we'll seed all of our fresh herbs. Our coriander's suffering a little bit, but that's all right. We've got a new crop coming. And so, yeah, we do all of our seeding in this greenhouse ourselves. In our indoor farm, the seeding is done. It's completely automated, for example. So this greenhouse is quite labor intensive just because it's a bit older. And the grow lights, just to promote the, like, proper germination of the plants and health in terms of plant growth. Yeah, but we also. You don't see them at the moment. We also have lights above the aubergines and then above the channels. It's Just when it gets a lot darker, they turn on. So in Montreal, like in Denmark, I imagine it's light at 10 o' clock in the morning in the winter and dark at 4 o' clock in the afternoon. So we try and prolong the light in the greenhouse by artificial light, but they're all LED lights, so they don't require a huge amount of energy. And one of the great privileges that we have in Canada, well, especially in Quebec, actually, not in Canada, in Quebec specifically, is that our source of energy is hydroelectric, so it's renewable. Whereas sometimes I have professors from Dubai or Qatar come and they're like, oh, this is a great idea. I'm like, yeah, but your source of energy is petrol. You have to think about that. Maybe you could use solar, maybe that would be renewable and regenerative.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So we try and think about our impact, like along the whole supply chain, not just what we're doing here in Montreal, but this is a model that ideally, you know, in a few years there'd be a loofah in every city in the world or something similar.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: But you have to think about the natural resources that you're using as well.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: What are the main challenges you guys face?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So finding kind of growing our greenhouses or building our greenhouses at a rapid enough rate to sustain the demand, because the demand is growing, especially like with the tariffs and stuff, it was obviously a very stressful situation for everyone, but it also made people a lot more conscious of the importance of buying local, autonomous and sustainable food systems. So we were very lucky to have a lot of support during that time.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: But also, is this still happening?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Well, we never know a little less, but I think people are, you know, every time it's in the news cycle at the moment, the news cycle is Iran and Israel, but if ever it goes back to the us and I'm sure that people will be looking for cities as well. It's part of the irrigation system. No, sorry, climate control system. The irrigation systems in the bottom of the building.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: How does it work?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It's all in hydroponics. So we don't have any soil. This might look like soil, but it's actually a substrate. And so we use. Yeah, a hydroponic system, which means that all of the nutrition the plants need is in the water. We use water soluble salts, like Epsom salts, and it just gives the plants everything that they need. The only caveat to that is that in Quebec, if you want to be certified organic, you have to grow in soil and we don't grow in Soil, we grow in peat moss or in coconut husk. The aubergines are in coconut husk, for example. And it means that we don't have the organic certification, but it's okay. We decided to have another certification that's called certified synthetic pesticide free.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Which is super catchy. Rolls off the tar. Love it. But we did a lot of work with our clients to educate them about that. And that's one of the good things about being online is we have all of the real estate, quote unquote, that we need to be able to give information to our clients.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. And how do you get out?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: We do a huge amount of marketing online, so through. Through social media and paid advertising. But we're also very, very present at events all over Montreal.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, I've seen you.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah, exactly. So, yeah, super present around Montreal. These are people tissues.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, fascinating. They can just.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: But you could say others wanted to start a similar thing.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: One of the main things to be aware of is kind of being able to convince that this is a viable thing that should be on top of a building.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: That it's a viable model and profitable as well.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. And then obviously we have the case study here.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: But there might be different considerations around energy source and cost.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yes, absolutely. So each area will have its own particularities. Also, the culture of the clients that you're trying to get. Montreal is quite, I think, eco conscious. City people are very tight knit. There's a lot of emphasis on community building and so it's culturally a good place to start a business like this. And it's also a place where people are relatively forgiving and understanding of mistakes. And, you know, a delivery is late if your bananas are squashed. Like people are relatively empathetic and understanding. If we were to do that in the U.S. for example, with the customer service culture there, I'm not sure people would be as understanding. So it's not just, I think, about the material aspects of where you're building something or where you're starting this kind of initiative, but it's also the cultural, human aspects of it.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, that makes sense. Makes sense. Who would you say are the main or most important stakeholder groups that you work with?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Our partners.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: And our clients.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Under this, you have the farm. Farmers.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: When I say partners, it's all our suppliers. We just don't say suppliers because that's a marketing thing. But yeah, we say partners. So all of our suppliers. It's farms, it's bakers, it's butchers, meat producers, it's people who do patisseries who are growers, whether they're in other greenhouses or urban farms or in a field. It's. I'm trying to go through fish producers that come from the east coast of Canada. It's fish producers that maybe have an aquaponic farm in the centre of a city. Yeah, Chips, baked goods, snacks, chocolate, loads of different types of fruits as well. So berries in the summer, peaches and stone fruit in the summer. But we also sell bananas and avocados and citrus, which isn't local obviously.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: But we kind of made that compromise with ourselves to say, okay, people are still going to go look for those products in the supermarket. If we sell them, we have to find the best possible option on the market. And so for bananas we work with a Montreal based company called Equifri who work with farms in Ecuador and Panama, I believe, to source completely sustainable fair trade bananas. And that's what we'll sell on our marketplace. In terms of the citrus, we work directly with a producer in Florida, have done for years and we don't go through a distributor. And it's the same for the avocados. We source directly from a farm in Mexico as well.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: How do you find these people?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: With a lot of hard work. Yeah, yeah. It's also part of the, it's part and parcel of why we have such a big purchasing team. Because it takes a lot of time to do all of this research. And then once you found a producer that looks good, we have this thing called like we call it internally emission test. But it's a criteria, set of criteria that you need to fulfill if we're going to onboard you onto the marketplace. And it's, you know, based on quality of produce, agricultural practices, ethics, especially when it comes to meat certifications, organic and all of that. And so we'll do a lot of that research. And once we find, find a producer that we like or they have a product that we would like to have on the marketplace, there's all of the kind of nitty gritty research of finding how they produce, what they produce. Is it ethically done? Do they have any certifications? What are their production practices? And then is it easy to work with them? Are they trustworthy? Are they open to doing marketing with us? So it's a lot of work to find these kind of jewels in our food system. But once we do, it just works for everyone, for us, for them, for our clients. There's a bakery in my neighborhood called Jimmy and she, we onboarded her during the pandemic because she was struggling to sell to people on the street and, and now she has a bakery like somewhere where she can sell direct to consumer in the center of Montreal. But she also has access to 40,000 clients a week through the marketplace. So it allows producers that maybe have a bit more production capacity to have access to a bigger client base than just what they have have you know, in their day to day and without having to do all the marketing for it, without having to go get those clients by simply producing something that's tasty and good quality, they'll, they'll build loyalty and clients will come back week after week through the marketplace.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, I can see many of.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: What you're giving them is almost a package deal also. Thing is, well, I would imagine her business has grown even physically cuz she's on there. Cuz people know now tried it.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Then they go have a coffee, sit down.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: She has like a. As well maybe a bit of a.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: A quality. Exactly. I'd like to think so. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: We do all the leg work.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay. I would love to. To film the little video.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Okay, sure.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Are you after that? Yes. This line.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I think we need to go to a place that's a bit. No.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Yeah. We can go to the end. Maybe that way actually. Yeah. Every time I think I'm going to quit, I get a promotion. I love, love, love the people I work with and I cannot say this enough but Caroline, the director of content communications, creative director at Loofah is an absolutely incredible boss and I've learned so much from her and continue to do so and she's given me like the opportunity to grow, learn new things. She I think trusts me and I trust her and she's built a marketing team. So people I work closely, most closely with of people who are fun and interesting and understanding and good at their job and collaborative and it just creates an environment that I think is very hard to replicate. So that's, I find very important, impressive. And I think it's a privilege to say that you like your day to day work but you like the people you work with and you like the company like it's, it's rare and I think it's a massive privilege. So I'm very grateful for that. That's why I said well that makes me very happy. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Good to know.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It exists.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, exactly. Yeah, it does. Okay. I'm thinking of background. I feel like this one. That's good.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: It's also nice to work for a company where you share values and you kind of feel, even if it's a drop in the water, that you're making a little difference in the world.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Selfishly, it makes you feel good.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So the first question.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Was, what is the name of the organisation? And a bit about the background.

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So, I work at Loofah Farms. We're a Montreal based company that was founded in 2009 and we build rooftop greenhouses to grow food where people live and also partner with hundreds of food producers to offer an online marketplace where all the food is fresh, local and responsible.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Do you want to introduce yourself and your role?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: My name is Emily Peloquin and I work at Loofah Farms. I'm the PR and communications coordinator. Great.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And what product or service do you offer and how do you contribute to improving the food system?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So we offer rooftop grown vegetables right here in Montreal, but we also partner with a lot of local food producers to offer an online marketplace where all of the food is local, it's fresh. There's complete transparency on the food chain, and it allows. Allows consumers to make really good choices in terms of their food while supporting the local community. So by growing food where people live and also supporting local food producers, we're trying to change the current food system and build a better food system where the supply chain is shorter, where there's a lot more transparency, and ultimately, products are so much fresher and better for the consumers who buy them.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Great. And what would you like to achieve with your efforts? What's your goal?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: Our mission is to build a better food system. And our vision is cities full of rooftop greenhouses. So we'd love a world where people eat the food that's produced where they live, and not imported from hundreds and hundreds of kilometers away. And to change that food system that's currently broken for a better one.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, love that. What obstacles have you encountered? How have you managed them?

entries.speaker: Emily
entries.text: So building the world's first commercial rooftop greenhouse was a challenge in itself. Whenever we talked about this project to building owners or municipalities or even friends and family, everyone thought we were kind of crazy. But then in 2009, 2010, 2011, we built this rooftop greenhouse, and it worked. And we proved that this is a viable project. It's a viable way of producing food right here in cities. And so we got a lot more support, and people are now very enthusiastic about this project.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, so we're debriefing Loofah Farms, which is candidate case 2. Astrid, first, can you just tell me what was your experience like getting in touch with the. With the initiative and how did you hear about them? How was, how was it overall? The contact.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes, I heard about them from Contact, who works in the same space in food system sustainability work, who is from Canada. So they. Yeah, recommended me. Loofer Farms. It was fairly difficult to get in contact with them. They are very busy. And I tried to email. Didn't really work. Then I found their communications coordinator's phone number randomly on a website. So yeah, called her and that worked.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: So.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, it was also. It was very difficult to get enough time with her. She only had an hour. So she was showing me around and I was getting to know her and she kept talking about Loofah. So I ended up just recording our conversation and we got through quite a few of the themes also because she studied psychology and was very. Had that analytical mind on food systems that we're kind of looking for with transactions outside of the monetary ones. So I got a lot out of her from that conversation. But it was. It was a very different interview than my normal ones as we were like walking around speaking. Yeah. And interviewing at the same time.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, that's. That's interesting. That's good feedback to know. So it sounds like. Yeah, it wasn't as much of a formal, like go through the whole list of questions, but at least you're able to get a lot of it informally while you were being. While you were visiting the site.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, exactly. I think it worked well with her actually because she. You could hear how she has memorized the whole talk about Luffy Farms and getting her a bit more casually helped to get more information that she probably wouldn't have told me if it was a very kind of formal interview like the one she normally does to the press. So in that way I think it actually worked quite well.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. Okay, great. And then just a question in terms of like the transcription then and what was actually recorded? Because we just did. We pulled out what was in the audio interview, which I guess is when you were actually sitting down with her audio.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I put the mic on her and we walked around.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, so that was when you walk your hand. Okay. Okay, perfect. Great. Okay, so can you just give me a quick overview of what this project is about?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes. It's essentially farms on top of buildings in Montreal. So it started in 2009 with four co founders and three of them have left. So now it's only Mohammed. She calls him Mo, which says a lot about her relationship with him and how the. Yeah, the company functions. They had a difficult time getting funding to build the farms on suburb roofs, but in 2011, they got the go ahead by a developer who was building a big warehouse factory, something a bit outside of the. Of the center of Montreal, and they started their first greenhouse on top of a roof. And this essentially has developed into a business where they have. I think it's four greenhouses now around Montreal. They sell everything through an online marketplace where people order their food in advance, and they only make as much as as has been sold. They also work with other producers. So they have a big purchasing team that vets people and finds people in the local environment or local area to kind of include their products into this online marketplace, and then they run the whole operation. So they have the admin, they have the distribution center, they have the delivery service. They essentially have all of the. The whole kind of chain from farm to when the customers get their. Their groceries.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Perfect.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Can you share what. What was, from your perspective, their kind of personal motivations and values for this?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So she spoke to Muhammad's most personal motivations quite a lot, because I think they also sell the story of him and of how it started. And he's from Lebanon, where he experienced the food system as being a lot more transparent than what he experienced when he came to Canada. So he wanted to take the knowledge from Lebanon and use it in a Canadian context, where he wanted to make this food system way more transparent to the consumer. So his idea was to grow locally within the city of Montreal and create enough communication and enough of transparency for the consumers to actually know what they were buying and know kind of where they got it from. So he really wanted to actually fix this broken food system that didn't offer food that we knew where came from and offer a local alternative to what the supermarket offers.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. Can you tell me now a little bit about their. The. The model, the business model? You know, how many people working there? How big is it? You said it's about maybe about four rooftop greenhouses. Now, what are their. Like. Did she tell you a bit about their funding situation? Maybe their major expenses, how sustainable it is financially, all of that?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes. So they're a B2C company. They have a small corporate team that she sits in. I got the senses around four or five people. So very small. They have a larger purchasing department that does all of their communication with the producers and the. Well, they call them their Partners. Besides that, they have mainly part time workers who work in all of their greenhouses. And these are people she doesn't really know, but it's like students and. Yeah. Other people who work with harvesting and packaging and making, you know, the, the daily kind of greenhouse work. Their funding situation is they are a for profit company and they sell goods for money. So it's kind of a classic business model in that way. The sustainability of their business I think is looking good. Quite good. They have expanded a lot over the last five years in particular and there's a big want for sustainability within Montreal. She was talking a lot about Montreal's mindset or the people of Montreal's mindset around sustainability and around their willingness or their acceptance of. Of businesses to make kind of mistakes and be along the journey. So it seems that they have quite a loyal customer base and they call them their loof, like loofah something. They have like a name for them as well. So it's a bit of a community being someone who buys groceries from loofah farms. Yeah. And it doesn't seem that this is slowing down at all. I think it's just growing.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, but you said they have. Do you say they have a low customer base or you said they just have like a specific customer base that is loyal to them?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, like a loyal customer base.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. Does she tell you at all about like what their major expenses are or like. So their, their funding comes all. Mainly from just the sales of, of the produce?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes, it does now. Yeah. And then major expenses is. She didn't talk about major expenses. No, she didn't tell me about this. Yeah, I would guess it's from their whole system of production and delivering and so on. Yeah, it's kind of like a supermarket chain, but just all online.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. So they have to deliver all of the food too.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yes, they have a distribution center as well. So they work a lot with small farmers. And what the small farmers really get from them is both the admin, the logistics and the distribution. So they run all of these different systems for that local small farmer. So they have. Yeah, they have the whole kind of production system within their operations.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. So I guess that's already getting us a little bit into the stakeholder mapping. So we have the farmers and you just mentioned a bit of, you know, what they're providing for them. Maybe we can go into the other stakeholders or if there's anything else you want to say about the farmers and the relationship.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, so I guess the two major stakeholders that she was talking about, Because I did ask about this a bit more. It was their customers and then the farmers they work with really as. As the two they work with and the farmers, they, besides what I already mentioned mentioned, they also get a high level of flexibility. So this is why they have a big purchasing department, is because some years their crop harvest will fail and they can't really contribute to the loofah farm online marketplace as they would normally. So the job of these, this purchasing department is to like, call them up and figure out, okay, do you have anything else we can sell? Do we need to hold off until next year? Do we need to find this product somewhere else and keep the good relationship going with that local farmer, even if they can't provide products to them? So it's a high level of flexibility for these farmers that loofah can offer.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. And I'm just curious, like, because they have their own rooftop gardens, their own produce right from there, but then they have the other producers who they are including in their online platform. Did you get an idea of, like, what percentage of their sales and their work is focused on their actual urban rooftop farms and. And what is more, on the network of farmers or other producers that they're including on the platform?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: It's a good question. I got the sense that the business is fairly divided. So one part of the business will focus on their own production, and then another part will focus on. On their partners and the other farmers and the other producers that they work with. Yeah, and like the daily runnings of their own rooftop greenhouses seems to be very kind of separate from this. But it all seems to be driven by customer demand. So the way that they get new producers into their products is by a customer saying, hey, why don't you have carrots? And they'll be like, well, we don't grow carrots in the rooftop because we can't. So let's find a local farmer that is good enough in terms of sustainability and values and aligns with all that loofah stands for and then get them into the. To the product selection.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: So I think the flexibility is the key word here, really. And the fact that they kind of collaborate with their customers as well on figuring out what kind of products they would like and how to, you know, then they figure out all the admin, like how to get them in, how to deliver the products and what the price should be and so on. So another thing that loofah also gives to the farmers and the producers is essentially seal of approval. So when you work with luffa you have to have a certain standard of sustainability and a certain set of values. And this is something that the farmers can also benefit from.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. And then the customers themselves. Anything else to say about that relationship? I mean, you said they have like a loyal customer base. Obviously their loofah farm is providing them with the produce. In exchange, they're, they're paying for this. Anything else to say about that?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: She was talking about how they believe that the right choice or the sustainable choice should be the easy one. So they see themselves as offering this vetting system for consumers so they don't have to go out and find that local producer and figure out if they uphold the standards that they want them to uphold. So I guess that's another, another thing they give them.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And then some, some say like they take their feedback and listen to them and talk to them and they try to give them a sense of being a part of a community, a part of the food system, not just being a paying customer that receives.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, sense of community.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, exactly. Yeah. And getting close to the farmers. Yeah. This is the whole point, right? Like shortening the system, shortening the supply chain. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. Any other stakeholders you talked about?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: These were actually the only ones that she really talked, talked about. Like in the beginning of loofah farms, they obviously needed a lot of investment in the idea. And this was, you know, a three year run before they got this investment. And she was talking about how the developer that owns the first building they built their first greenhouse on had said no to them in the beginning. And the reason why he ended up saying yes was because his wife and children thought that it was such a cool, fun project. So like he called them back and was like, oh, you know, fine, you can build a greenhouse on top of my building. Yeah. So it's very much, I think, I think the stakeholder grid should almost have a category of MO, the CEO's contact and network. Because he is, from my understanding, from what she said, like a real driver to why Lufa is both kind of a bit famous and has this good reputation because he's so passionate and people want to go on this journey with him of fixing the food system. Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: I mean, it seems like probably there's a really big, you know, network of. She only went into these two, but I'm sure there's like a very big network of stakeholders. Right. From various partners or even like you were just mentioning the buildings and how, what's the interaction relationship between even those spaces that they're using and how they're being made available to them. So you think it was just she didn't have the time or maybe like enough knowledge to go into that, but that there's more to it?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, I think she works mainly with the communication about Loofah farms, and that is from all the stuff I read about them before, is not like diving deep into the stakeholder network. So her job is not really to know what Mo does and his network and all of this. She is essentially selling the idea of connecting farmer with customer very closely. So those are her main stakeholders and obviously also the business is like main stakeholders. But I didn't get the sense that she really, that she knew more than like anecdotally the rest of the network they work with.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, okay. Did you have a chance to talk a little bit about what are the challenges that they face and then maybe also what are the enablers that keep them to continue and grow the project?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, she was talking about challenges of convincing customers that vegetables grown on roofs are a good idea and like selling this idea that's quite different to what they're used to. So this was a big, like, marketing challenge as well on getting consumers to understand, okay, is good because of the sustainability, because of the closed environment, because they can control all these things and yeah, grow locally close to where you live. So that's been like a bit of a. Of a challenge for them that they still have to kind of overcome as they sell to more customers. What really keeps them in business is also the fact that they are located in Montreal because water is so cheap and electricity is so cheap because they need so much water and so much electricity. This wouldn't be a sustainable model almost anywhere else in the world. Yeah, so that's like, that's a, a major thing for this case that she wouldn't recommend starting this in almost any other place in the world because it would, it would be too expensive with the. All the running of a greenhouse. Like, they have to have temperature control, they have to have irrigation, they have to have all of these different, very heavy, heavy electricity things running all the time. Yeah. And Montreal is the perfect, perfect place for it.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, that's a good, interesting, very specific point to this one. What about future plans or any developments that they actually want to work on now?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: She was mainly talking about how Mo is focused on making the food system transparent and this is what's guiding their work. So they obviously want to expand and want to keep growing because there are four growth company, but along that is the, the kind of main mission of also informing and changing consumers understanding of food and sustainability in general. But they didn't have any kind of future specific goals besides continuing continuing the business and continuing the partnerships and building out local partnerships and awareness of this.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. Do you think that this case has some more depth or uniqueness or future plans that would be interesting for us to monitor and come back to later or maybe like, you know, speaking to Mo in particular. But yeah, do you think this case is like something that stands out as one that we might want to come back to?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I think it's a super interesting idea of a solution of like how do you grow locally in a, in a massive city? And I know now they supply 2% of Montreal's food, which is quite big and I think they will grow. So I guess as like a case study of a solution that's possible probably with different electricity that. I don't know how to do that exactly. But there would be. Would have to be some adapting to other types of. Of cities. Yeah. But I do think it's very unique. I've never spoken to. Yeah. Such a successful company that has such a unique taken and growing food in a city.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, cool. Great. Any other insights or. I mean you, you did already mention in terms of like particularly interesting things about this case, the thing about the water and the electricity and how that makes it possible in, in that context. But any other especially important or interesting things to understand about this case?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I did think it was interesting speaking to. To Emily because she has been wanting to do a master's for years now, but she keeps staying in the business and she was saying that this is also because of the, of the work culture and how people are in the business. So I do think Mo is a large part of the success of this because he's clearly created a very collaborative and very kind of safe environment. Also taking a chance on someone like her who like, is clearly so intelligent. But she has a bachelor's in psychology and that's it. And now she is leading their communications. So it seems that he is keeping this small business startup passionate vibe within a company that's grown a lot. And I do think this is. Yeah, I don't know. He. I would love to speak to him. He sounds like a very interesting person.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay, cool.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: All right. Anything else you want to mention? I mean it is always interesting to know if you heard about any other cases as well through this one. Although right now I guess we're not going to go back to Canada right now, but maybe later any connections or interesting things yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: No, she didn't say. We didn't quite get to that. Okay. But I have a phone number, so we can always go back to her and ask. Okay. Super nice. Yeah. Let me just my notes, if there are anything else. Yeah. No.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. Thanks, Astrid.
